candidate_problems.text: How can we construct a non-arbitrary, error-correcting theory of 'law-space' that specifies which variations of constants or laws are genuinely possible, stable, and explanatory, rather than merely mathematically writable? This is the central unresolved tension because the conjecture shifts the problem from explaining observed constants to explaining the comparison class itself, but offers no criterion for delimiting that space without circularity.

What would count as a rigorous measure of explanatory progress in fine-tuning debates—i.e., how can 'error-correction' be operationalized so that anthropic, unificatory, and robustness-based explanations can be compared by their capacity to forbid alternatives, generate risky predictions, and expose themselves to criticism? The conjecture depends on this ranking principle, but it remains unclear how to formalize it without collapsing into subjective theory preference.

Is apparent fine-tuning mostly an artifact of current parametrization, or is complexity in fact rare across physically serious nearby theories? This remains open because the conjecture suggests that complexity may be robust under deeper reparameterizations or hidden constraints, yet we currently lack a theory of complexity across alternative laws that could distinguish genuine fragility from representational illusion.
